{{Short description="Energy flux vector of an electromagnetic field}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Poynting vector
| image = Poynting vector.svg
| caption = The Poynting vector <math>\mathbf{S}</math> represents the directional energy flux of an electromagnetic field.
| topics = [[Electromagnetic wave]], [[Electric field]], [[Magnetic field]], [[Maxwell's equations]]
| symbol = S
| SI_unit = W/m²
| formula = <math>\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{\mu_0}\mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{B}</math>
}}

In [[electromagnetism]], the '''Poynting vector''' (named after [[John Henry Poynting]]) represents the directional [[energy flux]] (the energy transfer per unit area per unit time) of an [[electromagnetic field]]. It is defined as:

:<math>\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{\mu_0}\mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{B}</math>

where:
* <math>\mathbf{S}</math> is the Poynting vector (W/m²)
* <math>\mathbf{E}</math> is the [[electric field]]
* <math>\mathbf{B}</math> is the [[magnetic field]]
* <math>\mu_0</math> is the [[vacuum permeability]]

In [[dielectric]] media, the expression generalises to:
:<math>\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}</math>
where <math>\mathbf{H}</math> is the auxiliary magnetic field.

== Poynting's theorem ==
[[Poynting's theorem]] expresses conservation of electromagnetic energy:

:<math>-\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{S} + \mathbf{J} \cdot \mathbf{E}</math>

where <math>u = \frac{1}{2}(\varepsilon_0 E^2 + \frac{1}{\mu_0}B^2)</math> is the electromagnetic energy density.

The theorem states that the decrease in electromagnetic energy in a volume equals the energy flux out of the surface plus the work done on charges.

== Plane waves ==
For a sinusoidal [[electromagnetic wave]] travelling in vacuum:
:<math>\langle S \rangle = \frac{1}{2\mu_0 c}E_0^2</math>

The time-averaged Poynting vector gives the [[intensity (physics)|intensity]] (irradiance) of the wave:
:<math>I = \langle S \rangle = \frac{1}{2}c\varepsilon_0 E_0^2</math>

== Momentum of electromagnetic field ==
The Poynting vector relates to the momentum density of the field:
:<math>\mathbf{p}_{\text{EM}} = \frac{1}{c^2}\mathbf{S}</math>

This gives rise to [[radiation pressure]].

== Applications ==
* Calculating power radiated by [[antenna (radio)|antennas]]
* Energy flow in [[transmission line]]s and [[waveguide]]s
* [[Solar irradiance]] — the solar constant (~1361 W/m²)
* [[Radiation pressure]] on spacecraft and particles
* Power transfer in [[transformer]]s (Poynting vector flows through the core, not the wires)

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Electric field]]
* [[Electromagnetic wave]]
* [[Magnetic field]]
* [[Maxwell's equations]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
[[Category:Electromagnetic radiation]]
